D.C.'s "DEADLY FORCE"

Jo Craven The Washington Post April 1999
Crime & JusticeData & Databases

In the 1990s, District of Columbia police shot and killed more people per resident than any metro police department in the country including New York, Los Angeles, Chicago, Detroit, and New Orleans. T...

In the 1990s, District of Columbia police shot and killed more people per resident than any metro police department in the country including New York, Los Angeles, Chicago, Detroit, and New Orleans. The Washington Post story, "Deadly Force," resulted from a close examination of the FBI's Supplementary Homicide Report. That examination revealed missing records in the SHR data. Conversations with the FBI revealed discrepancies in the data provided to the public.

Jo Craven recaps the in-depth investigation that ultimately relied on direct surveys of police departments for fatal shootings and woundings from 1990 to 1997. After analyzing the data, averaging various categories, and consulting criminologists, Jo Craven and the team discovered the high rate of officer-involved shootings in D.C., sparking significant discourse on police conduct.
